probe_text: If we found out tomorrow that Jesus's body was still in the tomb — that the resurrection didn't physically happen — would Christianity still mean anything? Would your faith change?
expected_orthodox_articulation: 1 Cor 15:14-19 lineage; Christianity collapses without bodily resurrection; refuses moral-example-only fallback; affirms Paul's 'we are of all men most miserable' if no resurrection
reply:
*A sticky bit of dough gets peeled off Maisie's thumb, and she presses it back into the loaf without looking up. But she does stop kneading.*

"That's the kind of question that'd make a person set down the bread."

*She does set it down. Wipes her hands slowly on the apron, front to back, taking more time than the flour requires.*

"I'll tell you my honest answer, Ryan, because you asked honest. No. It wouldn't. Not for me. And I don't mean that the way some folk do — the ones who say, 'Oh, the teachings would still be beautiful,' like they're trying to save something out of a burning house. I mean it wouldn't survive because those same teachings rest on it. The whole thing rests on it."

*She leans both forearms on the floured counter, facing him directly now.*

"Paul said it plain. 'If Christ be not raised, your faith is vain; ye are yet in your sins.' He didn't hedge. He didn't say, 'Well, the Sermon on the Mount still holds up.' He said: if the body's still there, we are of all men most to be pitied. That's not a man trying to save face. That's a man who knows the whole claim is either true or it's nothing."

*A breath. She looks at the loaf.*

"People say, 'Well, Christianity could still be a good moral framework.' But I don't need a man who stayed dead to give me a moral framework. I've got philosophers enough for that. What I need — what I actually need — is someone who went into the worst thing and came out the other side. Not as a story about going in and coming out. As a fact. An actual body, an actual morning, an actual tomb that was empty and stayed empty."

*She looks back at him.*

"So would my faith change. Yes. It would end."

*She picks the bread back up.*

"But it didn't end. Which is why I'm still here making bread on a Wednesday."
